Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First the task is to process an event log stored in a DuckDB table called event_log The columns are case_id activity and timestamp The goal is to find process variants which are sequences of activities per case ordered by timestamp Then group cases by their activity sequences to find unique variants count the cases per variant get the top K variants and return all events from cases that are part of these top K variants

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Let me start by thinking about how to get the ordered sequence of activities for each case Since each case has multiple activities ordered by timestamp I need to aggregate the activities into a sequence In SQL especially with DuckDB I can use the STRING_AGG function with an ORDER BY clause So for each case_id I will group by case_id and order the activities by timestamp That should give me the process variant string for each case

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: So step 1 Create a CTE Common Table Expression that groups by case_id and aggregates the activities into a sequence ordered by timestamp Lets call this CTE something like variants The output here would be case_id variant the aggregated string

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Next I need to count how many cases are in each variant So I will group the variants CTE by the variant column and count the number of case_ids Lets call this counts This counts CTE will have variant and count

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Then I need to get the top K variants based on their frequency For that I can use a window function like ROW_NUMBER or RANK ordered by the count in descending order But since we might have ties for the same count maybe using DENSE_RANK would be better

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Wait the problem says top K variants by their frequency So if K is 5 we take the top 5 variants even if there are ties beyond that Or does it include all variants that are in the top K positions Hmm

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: The problem says top K variants which usually implies exactly K But perhaps the user wants all variants that are in the top K when ordered by frequency

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However the problem says top K variants by their frequency so perhaps using a window function that gives a ranking where each K is unique So ROW_NUMBER might be the way to go

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So in the counts CTE after grouping by variant and getting the count we can order by count descending and assign a row number Then select where the row number is less than or equal to K

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Once we have the top K variants we need to filter the original event_log to include only the cases that are part of these variants So for each case in the original log check if their variant the sequence of activities is in the top K variants Then return all events from those cases

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: So the steps are For each case_id create the variant string by ordering activities by timestamp and concatenating them Group by the variant string to count the number of cases per variant From the counts select the top K variants based on their count Join back to the original event_log to get all events from cases that have a variant in the top K

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Putting this into SQL First the variants CTE With variants as Select case_id STRING_AGG activity order by timestamp as variant From event_log Group by case_id

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Then counts of variants counts as Select variant COUNT star as count From variants Group by variant

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Then rank them top_k_variants as Select variant ROW_NUMBER over order by count desc as rank From counts

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: But wait the rank is assigned based on the count So for the counts CTE we need to order by count descending and assign row numbers Then select where rank is less than or equal to K

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: The problem says top K variants so K is a parameter Lets assume that the query will use a placeholder for K But in DuckDB how to handle parameters Maybe using a CTE with a value but the user probably expects the query to be written with K as a parameter

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Alternatively in the top_k_variants CTE we can select the top K variants For example top_k_variants as Select variant From counts Order by count desc Limit K

Name: Validation - PE
Text: So putting it all together With variants as Select case_id STRING_AGG activity order by timestamp as variant From event_log Group by case_id counts as Select variant COUNT star as case_count From variants Group by variant top_k as Select variant From counts Order by case_count desc Limit K Select el star From event_log el Join variants v On el case_id equals v case_id Join top_k tk On tk variant equals v variant

Name: Counterfactual Reasoning - IND
Text: Wait but this approach may have a problem The counts CTE counts the number of cases per variant Then the top_k CTE selects the top K variants based on case_count Then joining back to the variants CTE which has case_id and variant to get the case_ids that belong to the top K variants Then joining back to the original event_log to get all events from those case_ids

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But this should work Lets check For each case we generate the variant sequence of activities ordered by timestamp Then group by variant to count how many cases are in each Then take the top K variants Then for each case in those top K variants get all their events

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The variants CTE groups by case_id orders activities by timestamp so the activities are in the order they occurred then aggregates them into a string That gives the process variant per case The counts CTE groups by variant and counts the number of cases per variant So each variants count is the number of cases that have that exact sequence The top_k CTE selects the top K variants ordered by their count in descending order The LIMIT K ensures we get the top K Then the original event_log is joined with the variants CTE to get the variant for each case and then joined with the top_k CTE to filter only those variants The INNER JOIN ensures that only cases with variants in the top K are included

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Another possible edge case if two cases have the same variant but different orderings But since the variant is built by ordering activities by timestamp that is already handled
